nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to high-elevation freshwater systems of Michoacán, Mexico, most notably Lake Pátzcuaro (~2,035 m altitude) and associated springs around Chapultepec, Opopeo, and Tzurumutaro. Wild populations are reported from littoral lake margins, slow streams, and spring-fed pools with abundant submerged vegetation and leaf litter providing cover.

decorPreferences: fine sand or smooth fine gravel substrate
abundant hiding places (small caves, slate stacks, PVC offcuts, ceramic shrimp tubes)
driftwood and leaf litter (almond/oak leaves) for cover and biofilm grazing
live plants are generally tolerated (Java moss, Anubias, Cryptocoryne, floating plants)
tight-fitting lid with all cord/hose gaps sealed
sponge-guarded filter intake to protect tiny offspring
waterline kept 3-5 cm below the tank rim to reduce escapes

diet.notes: Generally reported as an opportunistic omnivore-scavenger that readily accepts sinking shrimp/crayfish pellets, algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach, peas, carrot), frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, and small pieces of earthworm or blackworm. Tends to graze biofilm from driftwood, leaves, and rockwork between meals. Calcium- or mineral-supplemented foods are commonly recommended to support successful molting. Should not be fed copper-containing foods or invertebrate medications.

goodWith: small peaceful schooling fish kept above the substrate (chili rasboras, ember tetras, neon tetras, endlers, guppies)
small peaceful livebearers (least killifish, dwarf mosquitofish)
calm bottom fish that are too large to be threatened but not aggressive (small Corydoras with caution)
robust nerite and mystery snails (some sources note occasional snail predation)
avoidWith: dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina/Caridina — frequently predated, especially during shrimp molts)
larger or aggressive crayfish (will be killed or out-competed)
aggressive cichlids and large predatory fish (will predate the crayfish during molts)
slow long-finned fish (bettas, fancy guppies are at risk of fin damage)
loaches and other active bottom predators that can corner molting individuals

summary: A diminutive 4-5 cm freshwater crayfish from Lake Pátzcuaro, Mexico, prized for its bright orange aquarium morph; widely considered the most community-friendly true crayfish but still an opportunistic predator on dwarf shrimp.
careNotes: The aquarium-trade 'CPO' is a selectively-bred orange color morph of Cambarellus patzcuarensis; wild specimens are typically brown-to-rust with gray or blue tints. The orange line is generally credited to European (Netherlands) hobbyist breeding in the 1990s. The wild species is IUCN Endangered, but captive-bred orange morph stock is widely available and not subject to wild-collection pressure. Generally regarded as one of the hardiest and most beginner-friendly aquarium crayfish — tolerant of a wide temperature and chemistry window, easy to feed, and small enough that even adult specimens cannot dismantle most planted aquascapes. Has been reported to be highly sensitive to copper-based medications and snail treatments; avoid any food, fertilizer, or medication containing copper. Has been widely documented climbing filter intakes, heater cords, and tight lid gaps — a snug lid and waterline kept several centimetres below the rim are commonly recommended. Tolerates live plants better than larger crayfish species (Procambarus, Cherax); most rooted and epiphyte plants are left alone, though young or soft shoots may occasionally be sampled. Cross-source notes: adult size spanned 1-1.6 in (2.5-4 cm, Aquatic Arts) to 4-5 cm (Aquariumbreeder, Wikipedia, AquariumStockingCalculator) — final range 3-5 cm reflects the consensus. Temperature recommendations spanned 15-24°C (Aquatic Arts, AquariumStockingCalculator), 18-24°C (Aquariumbreeder), and 20-26°C (Garnelio); averaged to 18-24°C with broader tolerance noted in prose. Tank-size recommendations were tightly clustered at 10 US gal (~38 L) across all sources; rounded up to the nice 40 L value. Lifespan spanned 1.5-2 years (Garnelio), up to 2 years (Aquariumbreeder), and 2-3 years (Aquatic Arts); averaged to 1.5-3 years. The supplied secondary URL (theshrimpfarm.com root, and the dedicated caresheet page) returned HTTP 403 on access; Aquariumbreeder was substituted as primary and Aquatic Arts + Wikipedia + AquariumStockingCalculator as additional secondaries. The supplied primary URL (aquariumcoop.com/blogs/aquarium/cpo-crayfish) returned only storefront/navigation content with no extractable husbandry information.
breedingNotes: Generally reported as one of the easiest crayfish to breed in captivity — pairs spawn readily in stable conditions with no special triggers required. Sexual maturity is commonly reached at around 2.5-3 months and 23-25 mm body length. Females carry 20-40 eggs (occasionally up to 60 in large females) glued to the swimmerets under the abdomen ('berried'); incubation has been reported at roughly 3-4 weeks depending on temperature. Newly-hatched juveniles are around 2 mm and cling to the mother briefly before dispersing — filter intakes should be sponge-guarded to protect them. Cannibalism among size-mismatched juveniles and from adults toward newly-hatched offspring is commonly reported, so well-fed conditions and abundant micro-cover (moss, leaf litter, fine-pored rockwork) are typically recommended. Different generations should not be kept together once a size gap develops.

primary.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (Aquariumbreeder)
primary.notes: Substituted as primary because the assigned Aquarium Co-Op URL (https://www.aquariumcoop.com/blogs/aquarium/cpo-crayfish) returned only store navigation/product listings with no extractable husbandry content. Source of adult size 4-5 cm, temperature 18-24°C optimal (15-31°C tolerated), pH 7.5-8.0 optimal (6.5-9.0 range), gH 3-18, kH 3-15, 10 US gal (~38 L) minimum, omnivore-scavenger diet, conditionally-peaceful temperament with conspecific territoriality, high escape risk, adult molt frequency ~5x per year, juvenile molt every 7-10 days, sexual maturity at 2.5-3 months and 23-25 mm, clutch 20-40 eggs, 3-4 week incubation, IUCN Endangered status of wild species.

additional.name: The Shrimp Farm
additional.notes: Assigned secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden on access (both the article URL and the site root were blocked). Substituted with Aquatic Arts, Wikipedia, and AquariumStockingCalculator for cross-verification.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts
additional.notes: Secondary substitute. Cross-confirmed adult size 1-1.6 in (~2.5-4 cm), lifespan 2-3 years, temperature 60-75°F (15.5-24°C), pH 6.0-8.0, gH 3-25, kH 1-15, omnivore diet, generally peaceful temperament with predation risk toward dwarf shrimp and small snails, and high copper sensitivity (explicit warning to avoid copper-containing foods, fertilizers, and medications).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Cambarellus patzcuarensis)
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback for family Cambaridae / order Decapoda. Confirmed endemic distribution to Lake Pátzcuaro and associated springs in Michoacán, Mexico (~2,035 m elevation), total length 4-5 cm including claws, IUCN Endangered status, and that the orange aquarium-trade form is a selectively-bred color variant rare in wild populations.

additional.name: Aquarium Stocking Calculator
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check. Confirmed adult size 4 cm, temperature 15-24°C, pH 6.0-8.0, and minimum tank size 37 L (10 US gal). Tank-size figure independently corroborates the Aquariumbreeder and Aquatic Arts recommendations; rounded up to 40 L per project convention.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Adults have been reported to molt roughly 5 times per year (~every 10-11 weeks), while juveniles molt much more frequently — commonly every 7-10 days during rapid growth. Like other crayfish, the new exoskeleton takes a few days to fully harden, during which the animal is highly vulnerable and tends to remain hidden. Discarded exoskeletons are commonly consumed for calcium and should generally be left in the tank. Insufficient mineral content (low gH/kH, low dietary calcium) and unstable water parameters have been widely associated with incomplete or fatal molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Among the easiest crayfish species to breed; pairs spawn readily in stable conditions without specific triggers. See top-level breedingNotes for clutch size, incubation, juvenile size, and cannibalism details.
